title: Why we still phone our parents at the same hour

summary: Argue that not every dying craft should be revived with public money, and explain the distinction that should guide the decision.

title: Should cities slow down their public spaces?
summary: Argue whether cities should deliberately design streets and squares for slowness rather than speed.

summary: Explore the unease of statues, plaques, and memorials raised to figures who never consented.
topic: Why we keep building monuments to people who didn't ask for them

title: The case for letting some neighbourhoods grow old
summary: Defend the idea that not every district needs renovation, gentrification, or polish.

title: Should schools teach the use of obsolete tools?
summary: Argue whether students should learn to use tools and skills the world has stopped needing.
topic: Whether knowledge of obsolete tools should be taught

title: The ethics of inheriting a family business you don't want
summary: Explore the bind of being expected to take over a family business that doesn't fit your life.
topic: The ethics of inheriting a family business you don't want

title: Why some languages survive in households long after they disappear from the streets
summary: Explore why minority languages persist inside homes even when they vanish from public life.

title: Whether we owe attention to the elderly strangers in our buildings
summary: Argue whether residents have a moral or practical obligation to notice and check on elderly neighbours.

title: The case against efficiency in small daily tasks
summary: Argue that the drive to optimise minor everyday tasks trades away something worth keeping.
topic: The case against efficiency in small daily tasks

title: Why some apologies are worse than silence
summary: Argue that certain kinds of apologies cause more harm than saying nothing at all.
topic: Why some apologies are worse than silence

title: Whether public benches are a measure of a city's empathy
summary: Argue whether the presence and placement of public benches reflects a city's attitude toward its most vulnerable residents.
topic: Whether public benches are a measure of a city's empathy

title: The cost of a culture that mocks resting
summary: Argue whether the widespread tendency to treat rest as laziness has measurable human costs, and what a healthier attitude might look like.
topic: The cost of a culture that mocks resting

title: Whether grief should be visible in public space
summary: Argue whether public expressions of grief — memorials, rituals, and collective mourning — serve a necessary social function or whether grief belongs in private.
topic: Whether grief should be visible in public space

title: The case for studying things that won't earn you money
summary: Argue whether subjects with no direct commercial application — philosophy, classical languages, art history — are worth studying, and what is lost when education is reduced to workforce preparation.
topic: The case for studying things that won't earn you money

title: Why we trust handwriting more than print
summary: Argue why handwritten text — signatures, letters, ledger entries — continues to carry a weight of authenticity that print and digital text struggle to replicate.
topic: Why we trust handwriting more than print

title: Whether failed projects deserve their own archives
summary: Argue whether preserving the records of projects that were abandoned, cancelled, or never completed serves a genuine intellectual or historical purpose.
topic: Whether failed projects deserve their own archives

title: The ethics of remembering people the way they wanted to be remembered
summary: Argue whether the living are obliged to honour the self-image a person wished to leave behind.
topic: The ethics of remembering people the way they wanted to be remembered

title: Why some careers ask for moral debt the worker didn't sign up for
summary: Argue that certain professions impose ethical burdens on workers that go beyond what was agreed to when they took the job.
topic: Why some careers ask for moral debt the worker didn't sign up for

title: The case for being slow to forgive yourself
summary: Argue that rapid self-forgiveness can be a form of moral evasion, and that sitting with regret has real value.
topic: The case for being slow to forgive yourself

title: Whether we owe care to the buildings we leave behind
summary: Argue whether communities and individuals have an ethical obligation to maintain the built environment they no longer actively use.
topic: Whether we owe care to the buildings we leave behind

title: The forgotten craft of asking for help
summary: Argue that asking for help is a skill that has been undervalued, and that relearning it would benefit individuals and communities alike.
topic: The forgotten craft of asking for help

title: The case for boring infrastructure
summary: Argue that society should invest more attention and funding in unglamorous but essential systems such as water pipes, sewers, and electricity grids.
topic: The case for boring infrastructure

title: Whether old letters should be reread or burned
summary: Argue whether keeping and rereading personal correspondence from the past serves people well, or whether destruction is the wiser choice.
topic: Whether old letters should be reread or burned

title: Why some institutions need to fail in public
summary: Argue that when institutions such as banks, government agencies, or media organisations fail visibly and publicly, the exposure serves an important democratic function.
topic: Why some institutions need to fail in public

title: The case against celebrating productivity in children
summary: Argue that rewarding and celebrating children's productivity — output, efficiency, and measurable achievement — is harmful to their development.
topic: The case against celebrating productivity in children

title: Whether childhood homes should be preserved
summary: Argue whether the childhood homes of significant individuals — writers, musicians, scientists, leaders — should be preserved as heritage sites or allowed to be demolished or repurposed.
topic: Whether childhood homes should be preserved

title: The ethics of leaving an organisation that needs you
summary: Argue whether professionals have a moral duty to stay in a struggling organisation, or whether leaving is a legitimate and even necessary choice.
topic: The ethics of leaving an organisation that needs you

title: Why some cities deserve to be illegible
summary: Argue that cities which resist easy navigation and orientation offer something that optimised, legible cities cannot.
topic: Why some cities deserve to be illegible

title: The case for incomplete archives
summary: Argue that archives and collections that preserve gaps, losses, and silences can be more honest — and more useful — than complete ones.
topic: The case for incomplete archives

title: Whether grief has an expiration date in the workplace
summary: Argue whether organisations should set limits on how long employees' grief is accommodated, or whether such limits are harmful.
topic: Whether grief has an expiration date in the workplace

title: The ethics of saving someone from a mistake they want to make
summary: Argue whether intervening to prevent someone from making a mistake they are aware of is a form of respect or a form of control.
topic: The ethics of saving someone from a mistake they want to make

title: Why some traditions belong in the home, not the museum
summary: Argue whether living cultural traditions are better preserved through daily domestic practice or through institutional display and archival.
topic: Why some traditions belong in the home, not the museum

title: The case against efficient mourning
summary: Argue whether contemporary pressures to grieve quickly and resume normal functioning damage the psychological process of loss.
topic: The case against efficient mourning

title: Whether neighbors owe each other names
summary: Argue whether knowing and using your neighbours' names constitutes a genuine social obligation or whether anonymity in dense urban settings is a legitimate choice.
topic: Whether neighbors owe each other names

title: The ethics of taking notes during a friend's hardship
summary: Argue whether writers and others who mentally record observations during a friend's crisis are behaving unethically, or whether the observer role is compatible with genuine care.
topic: The ethics of taking notes during a friend's hardship

title: Why some public services should never become apps
summary: Argue whether converting public services such as welfare, healthcare, and crisis support into smartphone apps undermines the people those services are designed to help.
topic: Why some public services should never become apps

title: The case for libraries that don't lend
summary: Argue whether reference libraries — collections you visit but cannot borrow from — still have a place in an age of digital access.
topic: The case for libraries that don't lend

title: Whether streetlights should respect darkness
summary: Argue whether communities have a responsibility to limit artificial light at night, or whether safety and productivity must take priority.
topic: Whether streetlights should respect darkness

title: The ethics of inheriting a name you didn't choose
summary: Argue whether family names — surnames, dynastic titles, or clan identities — carry obligations that an individual is right to accept, question, or reject.
topic: The ethics of inheriting a name you didn't choose

title: Why some interviews should refuse the camera
summary: Argue whether audio-only or text-based interviews produce more honest, more substantive content than video interviews — and whether the camera's dominance in journalism should be questioned.
topic: Why some interviews should refuse the camera

title: The case for unfashionable kindness in tough industries
summary: Argue whether deliberate, visible kindness — in finance, construction, law, or competitive sport — is a sign of strength or a professional liability.
topic: The case for unfashionable kindness in tough industries

title: Attention as a moral resource
summary: Argue that attention — what we choose to notice, sustain, and offer to others — is not merely a cognitive capacity but a moral one, with ethical stakes comparable to time or money.
topic: Attention as a moral resource

title: On preserving what is dying: when is letting go the more honest choice?
summary: Argue that letting go can be more honest than preserving — that some acts of preservation serve the preserver rather than the thing preserved — using two contrasting examples and a careful concession.
topic: When letting go is more honest than preserving

title: The quiet violence of being unrecognised at work
summary: Argue the harm of being chronically unrecognised at work, show it through two concrete cases, and propose what an honest remedy would actually require — including what it would cost those in power to provide it.
topic: Being unrecognised at work — the harm and the remedy

title: The architecture of being noticed
summary: Argue that cities physically and procedurally distribute visibility, and that this distribution is unjust.
topic: The architecture of being noticed

title: On apology as labor
summary: Argue that apology, especially in service work, is a form of unpaid emotional labor — and consider what acknowledging that would change.
topic: On apology as labor

title: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose
summary: Argue that deliberate forgetting is a discipline of attention, not a failure of memory.
topic: The phenomenology of forgetting on purpose

title: Against the moral framing of rest
summary: Critique the productivity ideology that treats rest as recovery for work, and defend rest as its own end.
topic: Against the moral framing of rest

title: The hidden grammar of class in the language we use to thank people
summary: Examine how expressions of gratitude encode social hierarchy, and argue that the form of a thank-you reveals as much as its content.
topic: How gratitude language encodes class and hierarchy

title: On loyalty to projects that have outgrown their original meaning
summary: Argue that continuing to serve a project, institution, or cause after it has outgrown its founding purpose raises distinct moral and practical questions about loyalty.
topic: Loyalty to projects that have exceeded or changed their original meaning

title: When inheritance becomes coercion: the moral weight of received traditions
summary: Examine the line between preserving a received cultural tradition and imposing it, and argue that inheritance carries a moral obligation of consent.
topic: When received cultural traditions become coercive impositions

title: The aesthetics of the imperfect: why some craft cultures refuse polish
summary: Examine the deliberate embrace of imperfection in certain craft and design traditions, and argue that this refusal of polish is not nostalgia but a distinct aesthetic and ethical position.
topic: Why some craft traditions deliberately embrace imperfection

title: On living with people who remember you differently than you remember yourself
summary: Examine the friction that arises when others hold a version of you — rooted in the past or in a different perspective — that you no longer recognise as yours.
topic: The friction of being remembered differently than you remember yourself

title: The ethics of writing about a place you no longer live in
summary: Examine the moral tensions that arise when writers depict homes, cities, or countries they have left — and argue that the distance required to write clearly about a place is also the distance that distorts it.
topic: Whether writers who have left a place retain the authority — or bear the responsibility — to represent it

title: Against the cult of intention in art and design
summary: Challenge the assumption that knowing what an artist or designer intended is the most illuminating — or most relevant — way to evaluate their work.
topic: Whether authorial intention should govern how we judge art and designed objects

title: The political economy of being on time
summary: Examine how punctuality is not a neutral virtue but a practice shaped by power, infrastructure, and whose time is deemed worth protecting.
topic: How punctuality encodes social power and whose time is treated as valuable

title: On gentleness as a discipline rather than a temperament
summary: Argue that gentleness is not a personality trait one either has or lacks, but a practice that must be cultivated against the grain of competitive social life.
topic: Whether gentleness is an achieved discipline or simply a natural disposition

title: When honesty becomes a form of self-display
summary: Examine the phenomenon of conspicuous candour — the performance of honesty that serves the speaker's self-image more than the listener's need for truth.
topic: Whether radical honesty is always an ethical act or sometimes a form of self-indulgence

title: The half-life of a good idea inside an institution
summary: Argue that good ideas introduced into institutions tend to lose their transformative force over time — not through sabotage, but through the ordinary processes of bureaucratisation, absorption, and reinterpretation.
topic: Why good ideas lose their force once absorbed by institutions

title: On the moral status of attention you cannot give back
summary: Argue that drawing someone's sustained attention — in education, media, or relationships — carries a moral obligation, because attention is a finite and non-renewable resource.
topic: The moral weight of claiming and using other people's attention

title: Against the metaphor of 'balance' in working life
summary: Argue that the dominant metaphor of 'work-life balance' misrepresents the actual structure of a working life by implying a static equilibrium between separable quantities — and that better metaphors are available and necessary.
topic: Why 'work-life balance' is a misleading and ultimately harmful metaphor

title: When the language of healing harms the people it was meant to help
summary: Argue that therapeutic language — originally designed to reduce stigma and support vulnerable people — can, when misapplied or over-extended, cause harm by pathologising ordinary experience, reducing moral agency, and crowding out more adequate responses.
topic: How therapeutic language can harm as well as help

title: On the dignity of leaving things unsaid
summary: Argue that restraint in speech — the deliberate choice not to say everything one could say — is a form of moral and communicative intelligence, not reticence or dishonesty, and that the contemporary pressure toward total expressivity erodes the conditions for trust.
topic: The moral and relational value of deliberate silence and restraint in speech

title: The ethics of remembering someone better than they were
summary: Argue that the common practice of idealising the dead — softening their faults in memory and in speech — is neither simply dishonest nor simply kind, but raises genuine moral questions about the obligations of the living to the dead, to the truth, and to those still living.
topic: The moral complexity of idealising the dead in memory and speech

title: On the ethics of charisma in institutions that need quiet labor
summary: Examine how charisma functions as a form of informal power inside organizations, and argue that its rewards distort the recognition of slower, less visible contributions.
topic: How charismatic individuals extract disproportionate recognition from institutions built on unglamorous work

title: Against the language of 'investment' in human relationships
summary: Examine how the metaphor of financial investment has colonised the vocabulary of friendship, love, and care, and argue that this framing distorts what relationships are for.
topic: The distorting effects of economic metaphor on how we understand human relationships

title: The political asymmetry of who is allowed to be tired
summary: Examine how exhaustion is distributed unequally across social positions, and argue that who is permitted to express tiredness — and be taken seriously — is a question of power.
topic: How the social permission to express and be believed in exhaustion reflects and reinforces existing hierarchies

title: On the difficulty of being a witness without becoming a curator
summary: Examine the ethical tension between witnessing someone else's experience and the impulse to organise, interpret, and present it — and argue that this tension is not resolvable but must be held.
topic: The ethics of testimony, documentation, and the line between bearing witness and appropriating experience

title: The moral grammar of being on the inside of a decision
summary: Examine what changes morally when a person is on the inside of a consequential decision — not its object but one of its authors — and argue that proximity to power creates obligations that are not resolved by good intentions alone.
topic: The moral obligations that arise from participating in consequential institutional decisions

title: Against the framing of forgiveness as closure
summary: Challenge the therapeutic assumption that forgiveness necessarily resolves trauma or restores psychological wholeness — and argue that the pressure to forgive can itself be a second wound.
topic: Whether the cultural insistence on forgiveness as a path to closure places an unfair burden on those who have been harmed

title: On the discipline of refusing to be useful
summary: Examine the cultural pressure to justify one's time and existence through productivity, and argue that the capacity to resist usefulness — to think, rest, or create without instrumental purpose — is not a luxury but a condition of intellectual integrity.
topic: Whether the demand that individuals justify their activities through measurable usefulness diminishes human thought and life

title: When loyalty to a place outlives its hospitality to us
summary: Examine the emotional and moral complexity of continuing to love a city or country that has become hostile to certain of its inhabitants — and question whether such loyalty is devotion or complicity.
topic: Whether persisting attachment to a place that has turned hostile to one's identity constitutes meaningful loyalty or a form of self-erasure

title: The ethics of moral inheritance from people we cannot ask
summary: Examine the moral weight of inheriting ethical frameworks, loyalties, or obligations from deceased or unreachable predecessors — and argue whether such inheritance can be binding when no consent was given.
topic: Whether we can be bound by the moral commitments, debts, or loyalties of those who came before us when we had no say in inheriting them

title: Against the cult of clarity in difficult conversations
summary: Challenge the assumption that demanding clarity in emotionally charged or politically complex conversations is always an act of good faith — and argue that the pressure to 'be clear' can itself become a form of power over those who cannot speak plainly without risk.
topic: Whether the insistence on clarity in difficult conversations serves understanding or functions as a demand that certain speakers translate themselves into the language of the powerful

title: On the strange honesty of public failure
summary: Argue that public failure, precisely because it cannot be managed or reframed in the moment, produces a kind of disclosure that success and curated vulnerability rarely achieve.
topic: Whether public failure is a more honest form of self-disclosure than managed success or performed vulnerability

title: The political economy of who gets called late
summary: Argue that the distribution of late-night calls, emergency messages, and after-hours demands in organisations is not random but follows predictable social hierarchies, revealing who bears invisible costs that are never counted in official workload measures.
topic: Whether the informal distribution of after-hours demands in organisations is a form of structural inequality

title: When private grief is asked to perform in public rituals
summary: Argue that public mourning rituals — state funerals, commemorations, workplace memorial events — impose a performative demand on private grief that can distort, delay, or suppress authentic emotional processing.
topic: Whether public mourning rituals serve or obstruct those who are actually grieving

title: On the moral status of secrets we keep on others' behalf
summary: Argue that keeping a secret entrusted by another person is a moral act with genuine costs and complexities — not simply an exercise of loyalty, but a form of complicity whose ethical weight depends on the nature of the secret itself.
topic: Whether keeping another person's secret is always a morally neutral act of loyalty or sometimes a form of complicity

title: Against the metaphor of 'the journey' in professional life
summary: Argue that the pervasive use of 'the journey' as a metaphor for professional development is not merely a cliché but a conceptual distortion that misrepresents how careers actually unfold and subtly blames individuals for structural outcomes.
topic: Whether the 'journey' metaphor in professional development discourse is harmless or ideologically misleading

title: The ethics of inherited debts that no one named
summary: Explore the moral complexity of obligations passed down through families, institutions, or nations — debts incurred by others that nonetheless arrive at your door unnamed and unanticipated.
topic: The moral weight of obligations you did not incur but cannot refuse

title: On living near people whose ordinariness you envy
summary: Examine the experience of envying not another person's achievements or wealth, but the quieter texture of their daily life — and what that envy reveals about one's own conception of the good.
topic: The ethics and phenomenology of envying ordinary contentment

title: When institutional kindness becomes a form of containment
summary: Examine the line between genuine care offered by institutions — schools, hospitals, social services — and the form of control that can be enacted through the very language and gestures of kindness.
topic: The boundary between institutional care and institutional control

title: Against the assumption that listening is passive
summary: Argue that genuine listening is an active, demanding, and ethically laden practice — and that the cultural assumption of its passivity devalues both the act and the person performing it.
topic: The active and ethical dimensions of attentive listening

title: On the dignity of disagreements that don't resolve
summary: Argue that unresolved disagreements can be a mark of intellectual health rather than failure — and that the compulsion to reach consensus too quickly often sacrifices truth for comfort.
topic: The value of disagreements that persist without resolution
